questions.question_text: A laceration in sports medicine is best defined as which one of the following specific types of open soft-tissue injury affecting the skin?

options.text: A jagged-edged tear of skin from blunt force or tearing
A closed bruise under intact skin
A superficial rubbing of the outer skin layer
A deep narrow hole made by a sharp pointed object

questions.explanation: A laceration is an open wound with irregular, jagged edges produced when blunt force tears the skin, commonly over bony prominences; it differs from the clean edges of an incised wound and the superficial nature of an abrasion.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: A bruise under intact skin is a contusion.
Superficial rubbing of outer skin describes abrasion.
A deep narrow hole from a pointed object is a puncture.

questions.question_text: An incised wound in sport is characteristically produced by which one of the following mechanisms of injury to the skin during activity?

options.text: A clean cut from a sharp blade or edge

questions.explanation: Incised wounds have clean, well-defined edges and are caused by sharp objects such as skate blades, studs or broken equipment; they bleed freely but heal well with minimal scarring compared to lacerations with jagged edges.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Friction against rough surface causes abrasions.
Blunt collision without skin breach causes contusion.
Repetitive micro-stress on bone produces stress fractures.

questions.question_text: Which of the following Grade III (severe) muscle strain features most accurately distinguishes it from a Grade I or Grade II muscle strain clinically?

options.text: Minor stretch with no loss of strength
Complete rupture with palpable muscle gap and severe weakness
Isolated tendon inflammation without fibre damage

questions.explanation: Grade III strains represent complete rupture of muscle fibres, often showing a palpable gap or defect, severe weakness and inability to contract the muscle against resistance; Grade I involves stretch and Grade II a partial tear.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Stretch with no loss of strength is Grade I.
Partial tear with moderate weakness is Grade II.
Tendon inflammation without fibre damage is tendinitis.

questions.question_text: A comminuted fracture in a sports context is characterised by which of the following specific patterns of bone disruption after high-energy trauma?

options.text: Bone broken into three or more fragments

questions.explanation: Comminuted fractures involve the bone shattering into three or more fragments from high-energy trauma such as a direct crush or motor-vehicle impact; they often require surgical fixation with plates or intramedullary nails.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Bone bent without separation describes greenstick.
A single clean transverse break is a transverse fracture.
A tiny hairline crack is a stress fracture.

questions.question_text: A cricket bowler presents with sudden severe calf pain during delivery stride and reports a 'pop' sensation; which injury is most likely based on this presentation?
questions.explanation: A sudden audible 'pop' with severe calf pain and an inability to push off typifies Achilles tendon rupture from forceful plantarflexion against a fixed foot, common in explosive push-off sports like cricket bowling and badminton.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Stress fractures have gradual, not sudden, onset.
Ankle sprains involve the joint rather than the calf tendon.
A simple cramp does not produce a 'pop' sensation.

questions.question_text: Which of the following extrinsic factors contributes most directly to an increased risk of sports injuries during outdoor training or competition sessions?

options.text: Uneven, slippery or hard playing surface

questions.explanation: Uneven, slippery or excessively hard playing surfaces are major extrinsic injury risk factors — they alter ground-reaction forces, reduce foot grip and raise rates of ankle sprains, fractures and contusions among athletes.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Well-maintained surfaces reduce injury risk.
Appropriate footwear and guards protect against injury.
Warm-up is a protective intrinsic factor, not a risk.

questions.question_text: A badminton player presents with painful shoulder subluxation after a forceful overhead smash and reports a feeling of the joint 'slipping' momentarily; this best represents which condition?
questions.explanation: Subluxation is a partial, transient displacement of joint surfaces that spontaneously reduces, described by the athlete as the joint 'slipping'; overhead activities often stress the glenohumeral joint, producing subluxation with characteristic apprehension.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Complete dislocation does not spontaneously reduce.
Calcific tendinopathy causes pain, not slipping sensation.
Sternoclavicular joint is at the front of the chest, not shoulder.

questions.question_text: Which of the following best explains why female athletes show a notably higher incidence of non-contact anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) injuries than males?

options.text: Identical hormonal profile at all cycle phases
Wider Q-angle, different landing mechanics and hormonal effects on ligament laxity
Exclusively stronger quadriceps with matched hamstrings
Larger intercondylar notch and stiffer ligaments

questions.explanation: Females have a wider pelvis producing a larger Q-angle, land with relatively more knee valgus and reduced hip-knee flexion, and show cycle-related ligament laxity; together these factors elevate non-contact ACL injury risk.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Hormonal profiles clearly vary across cycle phases.
Quadriceps-hamstring imbalance actually increases risk.
Females typically have narrower, not larger, intercondylar notches.

questions.question_text: In cricket batsmen, a fracture at the base of the fifth metacarpal caused by punching or bat impact is classically known in clinical practice as which injury?
questions.explanation: A boxer's fracture is a break of the neck of the fifth metacarpal, typically from a clenched-fist impact or a direct blow on the outer hand; while named after boxing, it occurs in any sport with hand-on-hard-object contact.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Colles' fracture involves the distal radius, not metacarpal.
Smith fracture is a distal radius fracture with reverse angulation.
Pott's fracture is an ankle bi-malleolar fracture.

questions.question_text: Which of the following best explains why recurrent shoulder dislocation is clinically concerning for long-term joint health in a young contact-sport athlete?

options.text: Each dislocation increases ligamentous stability permanently
It causes progressive capsular laxity, Bankart lesions and early arthritis
It improves range of motion without any structural damage
It has no impact on future shoulder function

questions.explanation: Each recurrent dislocation further stretches the capsule, damages the anteroinferior glenoid labrum (Bankart lesion) and may cause a Hill-Sachs humeral defect, progressively raising re-dislocation risk and accelerating glenohumeral osteoarthritis.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Repeated dislocation reduces, not increases, stability.
Dislocation always causes some structural damage.
Future function is clearly compromised in recurrent cases.

questions.question_text: Match each sports-related injury in Column A with the specific anatomical structure most commonly damaged in that injury listed in Column B and choose the correct option.

column_b.text: Common extensor origin at lateral epicondyle

questions.explanation: Lateral ankle sprain damages the ATFL; tennis elbow is lateral epicondylitis of common extensor origin; jumper's knee is patellar tendinopathy at inferior pole; rotator cuff tears most often involve supraspinatus with infraspinatus.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Lateral ankle sprain always involves the ATFL first.
Tennis elbow is specifically at the lateral epicondyle.
Rotator cuff is made up of shoulder tendons, not knee.

questions.question_text: Match each eponymous bone injury in Column A with the anatomical site most commonly affected as listed in Column B, and select the correct combination below.

column_b.text: Carpal bone of the wrist floor

questions.explanation: Colles' fractures involve distal radius from fall on outstretched hand; boxer's fractures affect fifth metacarpal neck; Pott's fractures involve the bi-malleolar ankle; scaphoid fractures affect the carpal bone of the wrist floor.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Colles' is wrist-region; never ankle or metacarpal.
Boxer's fracture is metacarpal by definition.
Scaphoid is a carpal bone in the wrist floor region.

questions.question_text: Match each soft-tissue injury risk factor in Column A with its correct category (intrinsic or extrinsic) example shown in Column B, and choose the best combination.
questions.explanation: Risk factors are classified as intrinsic (within athlete — flexibility, muscle balance) or extrinsic (outside — surface, equipment); wet surface is environmental extrinsic, worn shoes are equipment extrinsic, flexibility is physical intrinsic, muscle imbalance is neuromuscular intrinsic.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Playing-surface factors are always extrinsic environmental.
Athlete flexibility is an intrinsic physical factor.
Equipment wear is extrinsic equipment-related.

questions.question_text: Read the assertion and reason regarding shoulder dislocation in overhead athletes and select the option that best describes their logical relationship.
questions.assertion: The shoulder is the most frequently dislocated major joint in overhead sports like volleyball, handball and cricket fielding.
questions.reason: The glenohumeral joint trades deep bony stability for a very wide range of motion, relying on soft tissues for support.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true; Reason correctly explains Assertion
Both are true but Reason does not correctly explain Assertion
Assertion is true but Reason is false
Assertion is false but Reason is true

questions.explanation: The shallow glenoid and large humeral head give the shoulder unmatched mobility but limited bony containment, leaving soft tissues as the main stabilisers; this anatomy directly explains its high dislocation rate in overhead athletes.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: The reason is an accurate anatomical description.
Both statements are well-supported clinically.
The shallow glenoid directly underlies high dislocation risk.

questions.question_text: Examine the assertion and reason about female athletes and ACL injuries in pivoting sports; then select the option showing their correct logical connection.
questions.assertion: Female athletes playing basketball and football suffer non-contact ACL injuries at a higher rate than male athletes.
questions.reason: The quadriceps-to-hamstring strength ratio is identical between the sexes at every stage of training.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true; Reason correctly explains Assertion
Both are true but Reason does not correctly explain Assertion
Assertion is true but Reason is false
Assertion is false but Reason is true

questions.explanation: The assertion about higher female ACL injury rates is well-documented; the reason is false because untrained females typically show higher quadriceps dominance and weaker hamstrings relative to males, worsening ACL loading during landing.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: The higher female ACL injury rate is established.
Quadriceps-hamstring ratios differ clearly between sexes.
Both cannot be true because the ratio claim is incorrect.

questions.question_text: Consider the assertion and reason relating to young athletes and growth-plate injuries in sport and select the option that best describes their logical relationship.
questions.assertion: Growth-plate (epiphyseal) fractures in adolescent athletes require especially careful management to prevent long-term deformity.
questions.reason: Damage to the growth plate can disturb future bone elongation and produce limb-length or angular deformities over time.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true; Reason correctly explains Assertion
Both are true but Reason does not correctly explain Assertion
Assertion is true but Reason is false
Assertion is false but Reason is true

questions.explanation: The physis is where longitudinal bone growth occurs; fractures through this cartilaginous plate can cause premature closure, limb-length discrepancy or angular deformity, making careful reduction and follow-up imperative in adolescents.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Growth plates govern bone elongation in children.
Both statements are clinically established.
Reason directly explains the need for careful management.

questions.question_text: Arrange the following biological stages of bone-fracture healing following a simple tibial fracture into the correct chronological order as observed clinically.

items.text: Soft callus of cartilage and fibrous tissue forms
Hard callus of woven bone bridges the gap

questions.explanation: Bone healing progresses through haematoma formation, soft cartilaginous callus, hard woven-bone callus, and finally extensive remodelling into mature lamellar bone over months; these four overlapping stages are well-established histologically.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Haematoma is always the first post-fracture event.
Soft callus precedes hard woven bone formation.
Remodelling is the final slow maturation stage.

questions.question_text: Arrange the following on-field initial assessment steps for a suspected fracture in a player into the correct chronological order according to standard sports first-aid practice.

items.text: Assess deformity, swelling and neurovascular status
Ensure scene safety and check consciousness
Immobilise limb with splint or sling
Arrange safe transport for definitive imaging

questions.explanation: Good first-aid practice is to first secure scene safety and consciousness, then examine the limb for deformity, swelling and distal pulses, immobilise it with a splint or sling, and finally arrange safe transport to imaging and hospital care.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Scene safety always precedes patient assessment.
Immobilisation logically follows clinical examination.
Transport is the final step after stabilisation.

questions.question_text: State whether the following statement is true or false: A dislocation should always be reduced immediately by any bystander or coach at the side of the playing field.
questions.explanation: Only qualified medical professionals should attempt reduction of a dislocation; untrained manipulation can cause fractures, nerve injury or arterial damage, and imaging is often required first to rule out accompanying bony injury.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Untrained reduction risks fracture or nerve damage.
Immobilise and transport is the safer field approach.
Imaging is often needed prior to reduction.

questions.question_text: Study the diagram comparing grades of ligament sprain and muscle strain injuries; then identify the grade in which there is a complete tear with gross instability and severe loss of function.
questions.explanation: Grade III denotes a complete tear of the ligament or muscle-tendon unit, producing gross joint instability or an obvious muscle gap, severe loss of function and minimal end-point resistance on stress testing — requiring imaging and often surgery.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Mild stretch with full function is Grade I.
Partial tear with moderate laxity is Grade II.
Grade 0 is not a standard classification in this system.
